romania.title: Nou investitor în România, cu cea mai mare afacere a sa de până acum
romania.summary: Compania austriacă de energie regenerabilă Kraftfeld Energy, cu proiecte de peste 1 GW aflate în diferite stadii de dezvoltare în România, a atras un investitor strategic într-un proiect hibrid de 126 MW/200 MWh pe piața locală, pentru care cei doi parteneri au obținut deja o finanțare de 90 de milioane de euro, relevă datele analizate …

romania.title: Noi informații despre starea de sănătate a lui Mircea Lucescu. Medicii nu au vești bune
romania.summary: Medicii de la Spitalul Universitar București au venit cu date noi despre starea lui Mircea Lucescu. Fostul selecționer al echipei naționale este în stare critică în continuare. A fost supus mai multor investigații astăzi, iar medicii nu au vești bune.

romania.title: Fulgy, fiul Clejanilor, a fost internat la Obregia după ce a amenințat că aruncă un bloc în aer. Au intervenit și negociatorii SAS
romania.summary: Polițiștii au intervenit în cursul serii de luni, 6 aprilie, la un bloc din Sectorul 3, după ce un bărbat a amenințat că a lăsat gazele pornite în locuință și că urmează să arunce blocul în aer. Potrivit surselor Digi24.ro, este vorba de Fulgy, fiul artiștilor Viorica și Ionică de la Clejani, care într-un final a fost internat la Spitalul Clinic de Psihiatrie „Dr. Alexandru Obregia”.

romania.title: FORME: un nou spațiu dedicat designului românesc contemporan s-a deschis în centrul Bucureștiului
romania.summary: FORME, un concept colaborativ inedit inițiat de IUTTA a fost inaugurat în cadrul Sheraton Hotel din capitală, oferind o abordare diferită asupra retailului: un spațiu în care obiectele nu sunt… Articolul FORME: un nou spațiu dedicat designului românesc contemporan s-a deschis în centrul Bucureștiului apare prima dată în Romania Pozitiva .

romania.title: 😂 Alegeri tensionate pentru şefia Academiei Române: Grapini o conduce pe Dăncilă cu 3 voturi
romania.summary: Noul şef al Academiei Române e pe punctul de a fi ales, dar scorul e încă foarte strâns! Potrivit unor informaţii din interior, Maria Grapini o conduce în acest moment pe Viorica Dăncilă cu 3 voturi, o amprentă şi un bastonaş. Cursa e încă strânsă, rezultatul putând fi întors de voturile academicienilor din diaspora, care […] The post Alegeri tensionate pentru şefia Academiei Române: Grapini o conduce pe Dăncilă cu 3 voturi appeared first on Times New Roman .

romania.title: De sărbătorile pascale, Kálnoky Castle Concerts continuă cu „Requiem” de Mozart reinterpretat
romania.summary: Recviemul impresionează prin intensitatea emoțională și dramatismul său Sezonul de primăvară Kálnoky Castle Concerts, intitulat Spring Stories, continuă pe 11 aprilie 2026, de la ora 19:30, cu iconicul „Requiem” de… Articolul De sărbătorile pascale, Kálnoky Castle Concerts continuă cu „Requiem” de Mozart reinterpretat apare prima dată în Romania Pozitiva .

romania.title: De Paște, 900 de vârstnici singuri primesc prezență umană, ascultare și speranță
romania.summary: În timp ce pentru mulți români Paștele este sărbătoarea revederii și a meselor în familie, pentru mulți vârstnici singuri aceste zile sunt printre cele mai grele din an. Singurătatea doare, lipsurile și sentimentul de abandon se simt mai apăsat tocmai atunci când toți ceilalți par... Post-ul De Paște, 900 de vârstnici singuri primesc prezență umană, ascultare și speranță apare prima dată în Fundația Regală Margareta a României .

romania.title: Start vot! Sute de mii de voturi vor decide Destinația Anului 2026 în România!
romania.summary: S-a deschis etapa de votare pentru cele mai apreciate destinații turistice din România iar publicul este invitat să voteze și să susțină destinațiile preferate finaliste în cele 11 categorii ale… Articolul Start vot! Sute de mii de voturi vor decide Destinația Anului 2026 în România! apare prima dată în Romania Pozitiva .

romania.title: 😂 Invitat la Summitul de la București, Trump a răspuns într-o română impecabilă: „Da’ o mu*e nu vreți voi?”
romania.summary: Invitat să participe la Summitul B9 de la București, președintele american le-a făcut o plăcută surpriză oficialilor noștri: le-a răspuns într-o română impecabilă. Asta arată că Donald Trump respectă cu adevărat țara și cultura noastră. Cu atât mai mult cu cât a folosit o expresie extrem de îndrăgită de români: „Da’ o muie nu vreți […] The post Invitat la Summitul de la București, Trump a răspuns într-o română impecabilă: „Da’ o mu*e nu vreți voi?” appeared first on Times New Roman .

romania.title: 😂 O mare minciună! Dacii de pe faţa ascunsă a Lunii spun că n-au văzut aşa-zisa navă Artemis II
romania.summary: La aproape 60 de ani de la aşa-zisa aselenizare, masonii şi reptilienii încearcă să ne păcălească din nou! Deşi comunicatele NASA susţin că nava Orion a misiunii Artemis II survolează în acest moment faţa nevăzută a Lunii, dacii care locuiesc acolo spun că ei n-au văzut aşa ceva şi că, cel mai probabil, totul e […] The post O mare minciună! Dacii de pe faţa ascunsă a Lunii spun că n-au văzut aşa-zisa navă Artemis II appeared first on Times New Roman .

romania.title: Bullyingul în adolescență: răni invizibile și cum pot fi vindecate
romania.summary: De ce vorbim despre bullying Bullyingul nu mai este de mult o problemă „minoră” sau o etapă inevitabilă a copilăriei.... Articolul Bullyingul în adolescență: răni invizibile și cum pot fi vindecate apare prima dată în World Vision România .

romania.title: Părinte Social
romania.summary: Descrierea postului: Părintele social crează un mediu de viaţă, îngrijire şi educaţie, caracterizat prin afecţiune şi stabilitate şi adecvat dezvoltării personalităţii fiecărui copil (în plan fizic, psihic, emoţional, social şi... The post Părinte Social appeared first on SOS Satele Copiilor România .

romania.title: De la un proiect Teach for Romania la o inițiativă europeană: cum elevii din Reghin construiesc „Leadership HUB”
romania.summary: O idee pornită din dorința de a dezvolta competențele de leadership ale elevilor s-a transformat, în doar câțiva ani, într-o inițiativă educațională care conectează școli din mai multe țări europene. Povestea proiectului „Leadership HUB” începe cu un proiect finanțat de Teach for Romania și continuă astăzi prin colaborări internaționale în cadrul platformei eTwinning și al […]

romania.title: Povestea lui Ionuț: cum sprijinul educațional schimbă viața unui copil
romania.summary: Află povestea lui Ionuț și cum sprijinul educațional și emoțional oferit de Fundația Inocenți ajută copiii vulnerabili să își descopere încrederea în sine. The post Povestea lui Ionuț: cum sprijinul educațional schimbă viața unui copil appeared first on Fundația Inocenți .

romania.title: Identități 2026: debutul artistic al noii generații de bursieri ai programului Tinere Talente
romania.summary: 36 de tineri artiști vizuali și muzicieni, susținuți de către Fundația Regală Margareta a României, se prezintă publicului pe 2 martie, la Elite Art Gallery, București Fundația Regală Margareta a României anunță Identități, evenimentul care marchează debutul public al generației 2026 a programului național... Post-ul Identități 2026: debutul artistic al noii generații de bursieri ai programului Tinere Talente apare prima dată în Fundația Regală Margareta a României .

medical.title: New test identifies active, infectious form of tuberculosis
medical.summary: Researchers in the UC Davis Department of Pathology and Laboratory Medicine have created a new tuberculosis blood test that can detect the active, infectious form of the disease. The discovery enables faster diagnosis and treatment. It also helps prevent the spread of tuberculosis (TB) by quickly identifying those who are contagious.

medical.title: Tinnitus linked to depression, anxiety in dose-response manner
medical.summary: Chronic tinnitus is independently associated with depression and anxiety symptoms, according to a study published online in Neuropsychiatric Disease and Treatment. Enes Sırma, from University of Health Sciences Gazi Yasargil Training and Research Hospital in Diyarbakır, Turkey, and colleagues estimated the prevalence of mood symptoms among adults with chronic tinnitus, as well as whether tinnitus severity contributes to psychological burden.

medical.title: Regional residents walk 75 minutes more weekly in highly walkable areas, study finds
medical.summary: New research published today shows that regional residents living in walkable towns and centers walk 75 minutes more each week, prompting calls for Governments to invest more in well-connected, safe footpaths to boost community health. The new paper, led by the Menzies Institute for Medical Research at the University of Tasmania, was published today in the Australia and New Zealand Journal of Public Health. It's the first of its kind to show walkability has a bigger impact on the amount of time spent walking in regional communities.

medical.title: Trial shows angioplasty reduces chest pain, boosts QoL in chronic total occlusion
medical.summary: In what is believed to be the first randomized placebo-controlled trial of its kind, patients with a chronic total blockage of a coronary artery who received a nonsurgical procedure to reopen the artery showed statistically significant reductions in chest pain and improvements in quality of life compared with patients who received a placebo procedure. The research was presented at the American College of Cardiology's Annual Scientific Session (ACC.26). The study was simultaneously published online in the JACC.

medical.title: DNA robots could deliver drugs and hunt viruses inside your body
medical.summary: DNA robots are emerging as tiny programmable machines that could one day deliver drugs, hunt viruses, and build molecular-scale devices. By borrowing ideas from traditional robotics and combining them with DNA folding techniques, scientists are creating structures that can move and act with precision. These robots can be guided using chemical reactions or external signals like light and magnetic fields.

medical.title: Hardly anyone uses this surprisingly simple blood pressure fix
medical.summary: Salt substitutes offer a simple, low-cost way to reduce sodium intake and improve blood pressure, yet very few Americans actually use them—even among those who need them most. A large national analysis spanning nearly two decades found that usage remains surprisingly low and has not improved over time. Researchers say this gap represents a major missed opportunity for preventing heart disease and stroke.

medical.title: Your DNA is constantly moving—and it may explain cancer
medical.summary: Scientists have uncovered a surprising secret about our DNA: it’s not a static blueprint, but a constantly shifting, folding structure that helps control how genes turn on and off. Researchers at the Salk Institute found that different parts of the genome loop and unloop at different speeds, with more active regions constantly reshaping themselves to support gene activity.

medical.title: This new test could transform UTI treatment with same-day results
medical.summary: A breakthrough urine test could dramatically speed up how doctors treat urinary tract infections, identifying the right antibiotic in under six hours instead of waiting days. By testing directly from urine—skipping the usual lab culturing step—the method quickly shows which drugs stop bacterial growth and which don’t. In trials involving hundreds of patient samples, the test proved highly accurate, matching standard methods in over 96% of cases.

medical.title: Scientists say this simple habit may help you lose more weight
medical.summary: A new study suggests that simplifying your diet by eating the same meals and keeping your calorie intake consistent could help you lose more weight. Researchers found that people who followed predictable eating routines during a 12-week program lost more weight than those who constantly changed what they ate. By reducing daily food decisions, routines may make it easier to stick with healthy habits in a world full of tempting options.

science.title: Green skepticism indirectly reduces intention to purchase sustainable products, says study
science.summary: Skepticism about claims regarding sustainability reduces consumers' intentions to purchase sustainable products by weakening two important drivers of green consumption: people's willingness to look for trustworthy environmental information and their anticipated guilt about making less sustainable choices.

science.title: A layered approach sharpens brain signals in optical imaging
science.summary: Near-infrared spectroscopy, or fNIRS, offers a way to monitor brain activity without surgery or radiation by tracking changes in blood flow and oxygenation. Light sources placed on the scalp send near-infrared light into the head, and detectors measure the light that scatters back. Because this light must pass through the scalp and skull before reaching the brain, the measured signal always includes a mix of superficial and cerebral contributions. Separating those signals has long been a central challenge for fNIRS researchers.

science.title: Why we're skeptical of the emotions we see on our screens
science.summary: If you've poured your heart out on social media about a political issue, it might have felt cathartic—but likely was not persuasive, Cornell research finds. Americans are skeptical of emotional comments they see in their news and social media feeds, political scientist Talbot Andrews and co-authors report in a new monograph, "Emotions on Our Screens," part of the Cambridge Elements in Politics and Communication series. Over six experiments involving nearly 6,400 participants, viewers questioned the sincerity of fear or sadness people expressed about climate change in simulated news reports, text messages and TikTok posts. Such comments were rated as less authentic and appropriate than more neutral ones—even when the observer agreed politically with the speaker.

science.title: New Artemis II 'Earthset' shot revisits Apollo 8's iconic 'Earthrise,' 57 years on
science.summary: NASA on Tuesday released a historic image of Earth dipping below the lunar horizon, more than 57 years after an iconic "Earthrise" image was captured by an Apollo 8 astronaut.

science.title: Tech can enable cross-species experiences, new research suggests
science.summary: Giving lemurs the chance to use technology to share control of sensory experiences with zoo visitors can help create meaningful connections between humans and animals, new research suggests.

science.title: 15 years after the eradication of rinderpest, lessons still ring true
science.summary: Permanently wiping out a disease is tricky business. Polio, measles, mumps—all have effective vaccines, yet they persist in certain pockets around the world. To date, the World Health Organization considers just two viruses as successfully eradicated: smallpox and rinderpest.

science.title: Oyster reefs stack up for shoreline protection
science.summary: Oyster reef living shorelines have been found to provide a resilient and adaptive alternative to conventional hard coastal protection, reducing wave energy while supporting oyster colonization.

science.title: Do you see faces in the clouds? Researchers examine pareidolia
science.summary: Humans are masters of seeing faces in any old thing—a handbag, TV static, toasted white bread. Scientists want to know why. A few years ago, as the category 5 Hurricane Milton bore down on the Florida coast, the internet noticed something strange. Doesn't the satellite image of the storm look a bit like an angry bald guy? Or a skull?

science.title: Quantum computing without interruptions
science.summary: Mid-circuit measurements are one of the biggest practical hurdles in quantum error correction on encoded qubits. Researchers in Innsbruck and Aachen have now proposed and experimentally demonstrated that a universal fault-tolerant quantum algorithm can be executed without such measurements. Using a trapped-ion quantum processor, the team successfully ran Grover's quantum search algorithm on three logical qubits.

science.title: Matcha model makes drug candidate screening more than 30 times faster
science.summary: Ligand Pro, founded by Skoltech professors and a Skoltech Ph.D. student, has presented Matcha, an AI-powered molecular docking model that performs virtual drug screening 30 times faster than the large co-folding models of the AlphaFold class developed by Nobel laureates, while surpassing them in both accuracy and physical correctness of the results. Matcha opens up new possibilities for virtual screening and early-stage drug development.

science.title: Hot-dry extremes could hit 28% of humanity five times more often by end of century
science.summary: In their current state, climate policies around the world could leave a significant chunk of the global population exposed to simultaneous extreme heat and drought over five times more often by the end of this century than during the mid-to-late 20th century.

science.title: Water on the moon? New study narrows down the most likely locations
science.summary: Water likely accumulated on the moon slowly over billions of years, rather than during one big event, according to a new study by an international team of scientists. The researchers, including Paul Hayne, a planetary scientist at the Laboratory for Atmospheric and Space Physics (LASP) at the University of Colorado Boulder, have published their findings in Nature Astronomy.

science.title: Earth’s magnetic field went wild 600 million years ago and scientists finally know why
science.summary: Hundreds of millions of years ago, Earth’s magnetic field behaved in a way that has long baffled scientists, showing wild and seemingly chaotic shifts unlike anything seen before or since. A new study suggests this chaos may actually hide a deeper pattern: instead of random fluctuations, the magnetic field may have followed a global, organized structure.

science.title: Ancient bone dice reveal 12,000-year history of gambling in America
science.summary: More than 12,000 years ago, Native American hunter-gatherers were already making and using dice—thousands of years before similar tools appeared elsewhere. These bone “binary lots” acted like primitive coins, producing random outcomes for games of chance. A new study shows these weren’t accidental objects but carefully designed tools used across many regions and cultures.

science.title: Mysterious Greek inscription may reveal lost temple beneath Syria’s Great Mosque
science.summary: A mysterious Greek inscription found beneath the Great Mosque of Homs could pinpoint the long-debated location of an ancient sun temple. Scholars now think the mosque sits atop a sacred site that transitioned from pagan worship to Christianity and then Islam. The find supports the idea that religious change in the region happened gradually, with overlapping beliefs rather than sudden shifts. It also reconnects the site to the powerful cult of Elagabalus, whose priest once became a Roman emperor.

science.title: Physicists just solved a strange fusion mystery that stumped experts
science.summary: Fusion scientists have solved a long-standing mystery inside tokamaks, the donut-shaped machines designed to harness fusion energy. For years, experiments showed that escaping plasma particles hit one side of the exhaust system far more than the other, but simulations couldn’t explain why. Now, researchers have discovered that the rotation of the plasma itself plays a crucial role—working together with sideways particle drift to create the imbalance.

science.title: NASA launches Artemis II for first crewed Moon flyby in 50 years
science.summary: A new era of lunar exploration has begun as NASA launches four astronauts on Artemis II—the first crewed mission to fly around the Moon in over 50 years. Riding aboard the powerful SLS rocket, the Orion spacecraft is now on a 10-day journey that will test critical systems, push human spaceflight farther than it’s gone in decades, and set the stage for future Moon landings and eventual missions to Mars.

environment.title: This 2-pound dinosaur is rewriting what scientists know about evolution
environment.summary: A nearly complete dinosaur skeleton discovered in Patagonia is helping scientists crack the mystery of alvarezsaurs, a bizarre group of bird-like dinosaurs. The fossil of Alnashetri cerropoliciensis reveals that these animals became tiny before developing their later specialized features, such as stubby arms and ant-eating adaptations. Weighing under two pounds, the dinosaur is one of the smallest known from South America.

environment.title: Scientists finally solve the mystery of yeast’s tiny centromeres
environment.summary: Scientists have uncovered how brewer’s yeast developed its unusually tiny centromeres, the DNA regions that guide chromosome separation during cell division. By studying related yeast species, researchers found centromeres that appear to represent evolutionary halfway points. These structures seem to have formed from retrotransposons—mobile “jumping genes” in the genome. The discovery shows how DNA once considered genomic junk can be transformed into essential chromosome machinery.

environment.title: Light-guided evolution creates proteins that can switch, sense, and compute
environment.summary: Researchers have created a method called optovolution that uses light to guide the evolution of proteins with dynamic behaviors. By engineering yeast cells so their survival depended on proteins switching states at the right time, scientists could rapidly select the best-performing variants. The technique produced new light-sensitive proteins that respond to different colors and improved optogenetic systems. It even evolved a protein that behaves like a tiny logic gate, activating genes only when two signals are present.

environment.title: Scientists discover hidden species among Borneo’s “fanged frogs”
environment.summary: DNA is revealing that many animals once thought to be a single species may actually be several hidden ones. But research on Bornean fanged frogs shows the line between species can be blurry—an important challenge when deciding what wildlife needs protection most.

environment.title: Lake Coatepeque
environment.summary: Set amid El Salvador’s modern, active volcanic landscape, tranquil blue waters fill a caldera formed by ancient eruptions. The post Lake Coatepeque appeared first on NASA Science .

environment.title: Scientists stunned to find signs of ancient life in a place no one expected
environment.summary: While exploring ancient seabeds in Morocco, scientists discovered strange wrinkle-like textures in deep-water sediments that shouldn’t have been there. These structures are usually made by sunlight-loving microbial mats in shallow waters. But the rocks formed far below the reach of light, suggesting a different explanation. Evidence points to chemosynthetic microbes—organisms powered by chemical reactions—creating the mats in the dark depths of an ancient ocean.

environment.title: Scientists finally reveal why mint feels cold
environment.summary: Scientists have revealed how the body’s microscopic cold sensor, TRPM8, detects both chilly temperatures and the cooling effect of menthol. The discovery finally shows how the sensation of “cool” works at the molecular level—and could inspire new treatments for pain and eye disorders.

environment.title: Bird droppings helped build one of ancient Peru’s most powerful kingdoms
environment.summary: New research suggests seabird guano helped transform the Chincha Kingdom into one of the most prosperous societies in ancient Peru. Chemical clues in centuries-old maize show farmers fertilized their crops with guano gathered from nearby islands, dramatically boosting yields in the desert landscape. The resulting agricultural surplus fueled trade, population growth, and regional influence.

mix_items.title: Părinte Social
mix_items.summary: Descrierea postului: Părintele social crează un mediu de viaţă, îngrijire şi educaţie, caracterizat prin afecţiune şi stabilitate şi adecvat dezvoltării personalităţii fiecărui copil (în plan fizic, psihic, emoţional, social şi... The post Părinte Social appeared first on SOS Satele Copiilor România .

mix_items.title: De la un proiect Teach for Romania la o inițiativă europeană: cum elevii din Reghin construiesc „Leadership HUB”
mix_items.summary: O idee pornită din dorința de a dezvolta competențele de leadership ale elevilor s-a transformat, în doar câțiva ani, într-o inițiativă educațională care conectează școli din mai multe țări europene. Povestea proiectului „Leadership HUB” începe cu un proiect finanțat de Teach for Romania și continuă astăzi prin colaborări internaționale în cadrul platformei eTwinning și al […]

mix_items.title: Povestea lui Ionuț: cum sprijinul educațional schimbă viața unui copil
mix_items.summary: Află povestea lui Ionuț și cum sprijinul educațional și emoțional oferit de Fundația Inocenți ajută copiii vulnerabili să își descopere încrederea în sine. The post Povestea lui Ionuț: cum sprijinul educațional schimbă viața unui copil appeared first on Fundația Inocenți .

mix_items.title: Identități 2026: debutul artistic al noii generații de bursieri ai programului Tinere Talente
mix_items.summary: 36 de tineri artiști vizuali și muzicieni, susținuți de către Fundația Regală Margareta a României, se prezintă publicului pe 2 martie, la Elite Art Gallery, București Fundația Regală Margareta a României anunță Identități, evenimentul care marchează debutul public al generației 2026 a programului național... Post-ul Identități 2026: debutul artistic al noii generații de bursieri ai programului Tinere Talente apare prima dată în Fundația Regală Margareta a României .

mix_items.title: Green skepticism indirectly reduces intention to purchase sustainable products, says study
mix_items.summary: Skepticism about claims regarding sustainability reduces consumers' intentions to purchase sustainable products by weakening two important drivers of green consumption: people's willingness to look for trustworthy environmental information and their anticipated guilt about making less sustainable choices.

mix_items.title: A layered approach sharpens brain signals in optical imaging
mix_items.summary: Near-infrared spectroscopy, or fNIRS, offers a way to monitor brain activity without surgery or radiation by tracking changes in blood flow and oxygenation. Light sources placed on the scalp send near-infrared light into the head, and detectors measure the light that scatters back. Because this light must pass through the scalp and skull before reaching the brain, the measured signal always includes a mix of superficial and cerebral contributions. Separating those signals has long been a central challenge for fNIRS researchers.

mix_items.title: Why we're skeptical of the emotions we see on our screens
mix_items.summary: If you've poured your heart out on social media about a political issue, it might have felt cathartic—but likely was not persuasive, Cornell research finds. Americans are skeptical of emotional comments they see in their news and social media feeds, political scientist Talbot Andrews and co-authors report in a new monograph, "Emotions on Our Screens," part of the Cambridge Elements in Politics and Communication series. Over six experiments involving nearly 6,400 participants, viewers questioned the sincerity of fear or sadness people expressed about climate change in simulated news reports, text messages and TikTok posts. Such comments were rated as less authentic and appropriate than more neutral ones—even when the observer agreed politically with the speaker.

mix_items.title: New Artemis II 'Earthset' shot revisits Apollo 8's iconic 'Earthrise,' 57 years on
mix_items.summary: NASA on Tuesday released a historic image of Earth dipping below the lunar horizon, more than 57 years after an iconic "Earthrise" image was captured by an Apollo 8 astronaut.

mix_items.title: Tech can enable cross-species experiences, new research suggests
mix_items.summary: Giving lemurs the chance to use technology to share control of sensory experiences with zoo visitors can help create meaningful connections between humans and animals, new research suggests.

mix_items.title: 15 years after the eradication of rinderpest, lessons still ring true
mix_items.summary: Permanently wiping out a disease is tricky business. Polio, measles, mumps—all have effective vaccines, yet they persist in certain pockets around the world. To date, the World Health Organization considers just two viruses as successfully eradicated: smallpox and rinderpest.

mix_items.title: Oyster reefs stack up for shoreline protection
mix_items.summary: Oyster reef living shorelines have been found to provide a resilient and adaptive alternative to conventional hard coastal protection, reducing wave energy while supporting oyster colonization.

mix_items.title: Do you see faces in the clouds? Researchers examine pareidolia
mix_items.summary: Humans are masters of seeing faces in any old thing—a handbag, TV static, toasted white bread. Scientists want to know why. A few years ago, as the category 5 Hurricane Milton bore down on the Florida coast, the internet noticed something strange. Doesn't the satellite image of the storm look a bit like an angry bald guy? Or a skull?

mix_items.title: Quantum computing without interruptions
mix_items.summary: Mid-circuit measurements are one of the biggest practical hurdles in quantum error correction on encoded qubits. Researchers in Innsbruck and Aachen have now proposed and experimentally demonstrated that a universal fault-tolerant quantum algorithm can be executed without such measurements. Using a trapped-ion quantum processor, the team successfully ran Grover's quantum search algorithm on three logical qubits.

mix_items.title: Matcha model makes drug candidate screening more than 30 times faster
mix_items.summary: Ligand Pro, founded by Skoltech professors and a Skoltech Ph.D. student, has presented Matcha, an AI-powered molecular docking model that performs virtual drug screening 30 times faster than the large co-folding models of the AlphaFold class developed by Nobel laureates, while surpassing them in both accuracy and physical correctness of the results. Matcha opens up new possibilities for virtual screening and early-stage drug development.

mix_items.title: Hot-dry extremes could hit 28% of humanity five times more often by end of century
mix_items.summary: In their current state, climate policies around the world could leave a significant chunk of the global population exposed to simultaneous extreme heat and drought over five times more often by the end of this century than during the mid-to-late 20th century.

mix_items.title: Water on the moon? New study narrows down the most likely locations
mix_items.summary: Water likely accumulated on the moon slowly over billions of years, rather than during one big event, according to a new study by an international team of scientists. The researchers, including Paul Hayne, a planetary scientist at the Laboratory for Atmospheric and Space Physics (LASP) at the University of Colorado Boulder, have published their findings in Nature Astronomy.

mix_items.title: New test identifies active, infectious form of tuberculosis
mix_items.summary: Researchers in the UC Davis Department of Pathology and Laboratory Medicine have created a new tuberculosis blood test that can detect the active, infectious form of the disease. The discovery enables faster diagnosis and treatment. It also helps prevent the spread of tuberculosis (TB) by quickly identifying those who are contagious.

mix_items.title: Tinnitus linked to depression, anxiety in dose-response manner
mix_items.summary: Chronic tinnitus is independently associated with depression and anxiety symptoms, according to a study published online in Neuropsychiatric Disease and Treatment. Enes Sırma, from University of Health Sciences Gazi Yasargil Training and Research Hospital in Diyarbakır, Turkey, and colleagues estimated the prevalence of mood symptoms among adults with chronic tinnitus, as well as whether tinnitus severity contributes to psychological burden.

mix_items.title: Regional residents walk 75 minutes more weekly in highly walkable areas, study finds
mix_items.summary: New research published today shows that regional residents living in walkable towns and centers walk 75 minutes more each week, prompting calls for Governments to invest more in well-connected, safe footpaths to boost community health. The new paper, led by the Menzies Institute for Medical Research at the University of Tasmania, was published today in the Australia and New Zealand Journal of Public Health. It's the first of its kind to show walkability has a bigger impact on the amount of time spent walking in regional communities.

mix_items.title: Trial shows angioplasty reduces chest pain, boosts QoL in chronic total occlusion
mix_items.summary: In what is believed to be the first randomized placebo-controlled trial of its kind, patients with a chronic total blockage of a coronary artery who received a nonsurgical procedure to reopen the artery showed statistically significant reductions in chest pain and improvements in quality of life compared with patients who received a placebo procedure. The research was presented at the American College of Cardiology's Annual Scientific Session (ACC.26). The study was simultaneously published online in the JACC.

mix_items.title: DNA robots could deliver drugs and hunt viruses inside your body
mix_items.summary: DNA robots are emerging as tiny programmable machines that could one day deliver drugs, hunt viruses, and build molecular-scale devices. By borrowing ideas from traditional robotics and combining them with DNA folding techniques, scientists are creating structures that can move and act with precision. These robots can be guided using chemical reactions or external signals like light and magnetic fields.

mix_items.title: Hardly anyone uses this surprisingly simple blood pressure fix
mix_items.summary: Salt substitutes offer a simple, low-cost way to reduce sodium intake and improve blood pressure, yet very few Americans actually use them—even among those who need them most. A large national analysis spanning nearly two decades found that usage remains surprisingly low and has not improved over time. Researchers say this gap represents a major missed opportunity for preventing heart disease and stroke.

mix_items.title: Your DNA is constantly moving—and it may explain cancer
mix_items.summary: Scientists have uncovered a surprising secret about our DNA: it’s not a static blueprint, but a constantly shifting, folding structure that helps control how genes turn on and off. Researchers at the Salk Institute found that different parts of the genome loop and unloop at different speeds, with more active regions constantly reshaping themselves to support gene activity.

mix_items.title: This new test could transform UTI treatment with same-day results
mix_items.summary: A breakthrough urine test could dramatically speed up how doctors treat urinary tract infections, identifying the right antibiotic in under six hours instead of waiting days. By testing directly from urine—skipping the usual lab culturing step—the method quickly shows which drugs stop bacterial growth and which don’t. In trials involving hundreds of patient samples, the test proved highly accurate, matching standard methods in over 96% of cases.

mix_items.title: Scientists say this simple habit may help you lose more weight
mix_items.summary: A new study suggests that simplifying your diet by eating the same meals and keeping your calorie intake consistent could help you lose more weight. Researchers found that people who followed predictable eating routines during a 12-week program lost more weight than those who constantly changed what they ate. By reducing daily food decisions, routines may make it easier to stick with healthy habits in a world full of tempting options.

mix_items.title: Earth’s magnetic field went wild 600 million years ago and scientists finally know why
mix_items.summary: Hundreds of millions of years ago, Earth’s magnetic field behaved in a way that has long baffled scientists, showing wild and seemingly chaotic shifts unlike anything seen before or since. A new study suggests this chaos may actually hide a deeper pattern: instead of random fluctuations, the magnetic field may have followed a global, organized structure.

mix_items.title: Ancient bone dice reveal 12,000-year history of gambling in America
mix_items.summary: More than 12,000 years ago, Native American hunter-gatherers were already making and using dice—thousands of years before similar tools appeared elsewhere. These bone “binary lots” acted like primitive coins, producing random outcomes for games of chance. A new study shows these weren’t accidental objects but carefully designed tools used across many regions and cultures.

mix_items.title: Mysterious Greek inscription may reveal lost temple beneath Syria’s Great Mosque
mix_items.summary: A mysterious Greek inscription found beneath the Great Mosque of Homs could pinpoint the long-debated location of an ancient sun temple. Scholars now think the mosque sits atop a sacred site that transitioned from pagan worship to Christianity and then Islam. The find supports the idea that religious change in the region happened gradually, with overlapping beliefs rather than sudden shifts. It also reconnects the site to the powerful cult of Elagabalus, whose priest once became a Roman emperor.

mix_items.title: Physicists just solved a strange fusion mystery that stumped experts
mix_items.summary: Fusion scientists have solved a long-standing mystery inside tokamaks, the donut-shaped machines designed to harness fusion energy. For years, experiments showed that escaping plasma particles hit one side of the exhaust system far more than the other, but simulations couldn’t explain why. Now, researchers have discovered that the rotation of the plasma itself plays a crucial role—working together with sideways particle drift to create the imbalance.

mix_items.title: NASA launches Artemis II for first crewed Moon flyby in 50 years
mix_items.summary: A new era of lunar exploration has begun as NASA launches four astronauts on Artemis II—the first crewed mission to fly around the Moon in over 50 years. Riding aboard the powerful SLS rocket, the Orion spacecraft is now on a 10-day journey that will test critical systems, push human spaceflight farther than it’s gone in decades, and set the stage for future Moon landings and eventual missions to Mars.

mix_items.title: This 2-pound dinosaur is rewriting what scientists know about evolution
mix_items.summary: A nearly complete dinosaur skeleton discovered in Patagonia is helping scientists crack the mystery of alvarezsaurs, a bizarre group of bird-like dinosaurs. The fossil of Alnashetri cerropoliciensis reveals that these animals became tiny before developing their later specialized features, such as stubby arms and ant-eating adaptations. Weighing under two pounds, the dinosaur is one of the smallest known from South America.

mix_items.title: Scientists finally solve the mystery of yeast’s tiny centromeres
mix_items.summary: Scientists have uncovered how brewer’s yeast developed its unusually tiny centromeres, the DNA regions that guide chromosome separation during cell division. By studying related yeast species, researchers found centromeres that appear to represent evolutionary halfway points. These structures seem to have formed from retrotransposons—mobile “jumping genes” in the genome. The discovery shows how DNA once considered genomic junk can be transformed into essential chromosome machinery.

mix_items.title: Light-guided evolution creates proteins that can switch, sense, and compute
mix_items.summary: Researchers have created a method called optovolution that uses light to guide the evolution of proteins with dynamic behaviors. By engineering yeast cells so their survival depended on proteins switching states at the right time, scientists could rapidly select the best-performing variants. The technique produced new light-sensitive proteins that respond to different colors and improved optogenetic systems. It even evolved a protein that behaves like a tiny logic gate, activating genes only when two signals are present.

mix_items.title: Scientists discover hidden species among Borneo’s “fanged frogs”
mix_items.summary: DNA is revealing that many animals once thought to be a single species may actually be several hidden ones. But research on Bornean fanged frogs shows the line between species can be blurry—an important challenge when deciding what wildlife needs protection most.

mix_items.title: Lake Coatepeque
mix_items.summary: Set amid El Salvador’s modern, active volcanic landscape, tranquil blue waters fill a caldera formed by ancient eruptions. The post Lake Coatepeque appeared first on NASA Science .

mix_items.title: Scientists stunned to find signs of ancient life in a place no one expected
mix_items.summary: While exploring ancient seabeds in Morocco, scientists discovered strange wrinkle-like textures in deep-water sediments that shouldn’t have been there. These structures are usually made by sunlight-loving microbial mats in shallow waters. But the rocks formed far below the reach of light, suggesting a different explanation. Evidence points to chemosynthetic microbes—organisms powered by chemical reactions—creating the mats in the dark depths of an ancient ocean.

mix_items.title: Scientists finally reveal why mint feels cold
mix_items.summary: Scientists have revealed how the body’s microscopic cold sensor, TRPM8, detects both chilly temperatures and the cooling effect of menthol. The discovery finally shows how the sensation of “cool” works at the molecular level—and could inspire new treatments for pain and eye disorders.

mix_items.title: Bird droppings helped build one of ancient Peru’s most powerful kingdoms
mix_items.summary: New research suggests seabird guano helped transform the Chincha Kingdom into one of the most prosperous societies in ancient Peru. Chemical clues in centuries-old maize show farmers fertilized their crops with guano gathered from nearby islands, dramatically boosting yields in the desert landscape. The resulting agricultural surplus fueled trade, population growth, and regional influence.

joke_ro.text: M-am apucat de citit. Dupa 5 pagini am luat pauza de reflectie de 2 ore.

satire_ro.title: Realitatea Plus se închide. Călin Georgescu se întoarce pe Minimax
